objects.name: CyberNetSec.io
objects.description: CyberNetSec.io is a cybersecurity intelligence platform providing timely, accurate, deduplicated, and actionable threat intelligence for security professionals. The platform aggregates, deduplicates, and enriches threat data from diverse public sources, mapping all threats to MITRE ATT&CK and D3FEND frameworks with operationally-focused analysis, detection guidance, and mitigation recommendations. Operated by security professionals, for the security community.

objects.name: Jason Gomes
objects.description: Cybersecurity practitioner and founder of CyberNetSec.io with over 10 years of specialized experience in security operations, threat intelligence, incident response, and security automation. Expertise spans SOAR/XSOAR orchestration, threat intelligence platforms, SIEM/UEBA analytics, and cyber fusion centers.

objects.name: Kimsuky
objects.description: A North Korean state-sponsored threat actor involved in a campaign targeting South Korean organizations using LNK files and GitHub for C2.
goals: Espionage and long-term intelligence gathering against South Korean targets.

objects.name: Lazarus Group
objects.description: A North Korean state-sponsored threat actor suspected to be involved in a campaign targeting South Korean organizations using LNK files and GitHub for C2.
goals: Espionage and long-term intelligence gathering against South Korean targets.

objects.name: APT37
objects.description: A North Korean state-sponsored threat actor suspected to be involved in a campaign targeting South Korean organizations using LNK files and GitHub for C2.
goals: Espionage and long-term intelligence gathering against South Korean targets.

objects.name: Malicious File
objects.description: Malicious Windows shortcut (.lnk) files, disguised with icons and names of common document types, were delivered via phishing to trick users into execution.

objects.name: PowerShell
objects.description: A hidden PowerShell script was launched from a malicious LNK file to perform C2 communications and execute commands.

objects.name: Bidirectional Communication
objects.description: The malware abused the legitimate GitHub service as a C2 channel, posting stolen data to and pulling new commands from attacker-controlled repositories.

objects.name: Obfuscated Files or Information
objects.description: The malicious LNK file contained obfuscated commands that launched the PowerShell payload, and recent versions have shown increased obfuscation.

objects.name: PowerShell C2 Implant
objects.description: A PowerShell-based payload delivered by a malicious LNK file. It collects system information and uses GitHub for command and control, exfiltrating data and downloading further commands or payloads.

objects.name: XenoRAT
objects.description: A Remote Access Trojan (RAT) that can be delivered as a secondary payload via the GitHub C2 channel for long-term control and extensive data theft.

objects.name: PowerShell
objects.description: Used as the execution engine for the primary malware payload, which communicates with the GitHub C2 and executes received commands.

objects.name: GitHub C2 Repositories
objects.description: Attacker-controlled GitHub repositories used as a command-and-control channel. The malware posts stolen data to and pulls new commands from these repositories, camouflaging the traffic as legitimate web activity.

objects.name: GitHub C2 Domain api.github.com
objects.description: Detects network connections to api.github.com from processes other than common developer tools or browsers. This activity is associated with the North Korean campaign using GitHub for C2.
objects.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: Anomalous Process Connecting to GitHub API
id: 2b1d6a0f-8c7e-4b5a-9f1d-3c2e1b0a9f8d
status: experimental
description: Detects processes that are not common developer tools or browsers connecting to api.github.com, which could indicate abuse of GitHub for C2 communications.
references:
  - https://gbhackers.com/github-backed-malware-spread-via-lnk-files-in-south-korea/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-04-06
tags:
  - attack.t1102.002
  - attack.command_and_control
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate developer tools, update clients, or scripts that interact with the GitHub API.
level: medium
logsource:
  category: network_connection
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    DestinationHostname: 'api.github.com'
    Initiated: 'true'
  filter:
    Image|endswith:
      - '\git.exe'
      - '\gh.exe'
      - '\Code.exe'
      - '\chrome.exe'
      - '\firefox.exe'
      - '\msedge.exe'
  condition: selection and not filter

objects.name: PowerShell Execution from LNK File
objects.description: Detects the execution of PowerShell from a malicious LNK file, which was the initial execution vector in this campaign.
objects.pattern:
title: PowerShell Execution from LNK File
id: 5e8f1c2a-3b4d-5e6f-7a8b-9c0d1e2f3a4b
status: experimental
description: Detects the execution of PowerShell launched from a Windows shortcut (LNK) file, a technique used in a recent North Korean campaign.
references:
  - https://gbhackers.com/github-backed-malware-spread-via-lnk-files-in-south-korea/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-04-06
tags:
  - attack.t1204.002
  - attack.t1059.001
  - attack.execution
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate administrative scripts launched via shortcuts, though this is uncommon.
level: high
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    ParentImage|endswith: '.lnk'
    Image|endswith: '\powershell.exe'
  condition: selection

objects.name: PowerShell GitHub C2 Script Block
objects.description: Detects PowerShell script block logging events that show scripts making web requests to api.github.com, a key behavior of the C2 implant.
objects.pattern:
title: PowerShell Script Making Web Request to GitHub API
id: 9a8b7c6d-5e4f-3a2b-1c0d-9f8e7d6c5b4a
status: experimental
description: 'Detects PowerShell script blocks (via Event ID 4104) containing web requests to api.github.com, indicating potential C2 activity.'
references:
  - https://gbhackers.com/github-backed-malware-spread-via-lnk-files-in-south-korea/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-04-06
tags:
  - attack.t1102.002
  - attack.t1059.001
  - attack.command_and_control
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate administrative or developer scripts that interact with the GitHub API.
level: high
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: powershell
  category: ps_script
detection:
  selection:
    ScriptBlockText|contains:
      - 'api.github.com'
      - 'Invoke-RestMethod'
      - 'Invoke-WebRequest'
  condition: all of selection

objects.name: North Korean Hackers Abuse GitHub for C2 in Campaign Targeting South Korea
objects.description:
## Executive Summary

Security researchers have identified an ongoing, stealthy malware campaign targeting organizations in South Korea, with strong evidence linking it to North Korean state-sponsored threat actors such as **[Kimsuky](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0094/)** and **[Lazarus Group](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0032/)**. The campaign leverages malicious Windows shortcut (`.lnk`) files, often delivered via phishing, as the initial infection vector. Upon execution, a hidden **[PowerShell](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059/001/)** script is run, which uses **[GitHub](https://github.com/)** as a command-and-control (C2) platform. By abusing the legitimate and widely trusted GitHub service, the attackers' C2 communications are effectively camouflaged within normal network traffic, making detection extremely difficult. The ultimate goal of the campaign appears to be espionage and long-term intelligence gathering.

---

## Threat Overview

**Attribution:** Suspected North Korean state-sponsored groups (Kimsuky, APT37, Lazarus).

This campaign demonstrates the continuing evolution of North Korean TTPs, focusing on stealth and the abuse of legitimate services:

- **Initial Access:** The use of malicious LNK files ([`T1204.002 - Malicious File`](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1204/002/)) delivered via phishing. These files are disguised with icons and names of common document types (e.g., PDFs) to trick users.
- **Execution:** The LNK file contains obfuscated commands that launch a PowerShell script to perform the main malicious activities.
- **Command and Control:** The most notable feature is the use of GitHub as a C2 channel ([`T1102.002 - Web Service`](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1102/002/)). The PowerShell script communicates with attacker-controlled GitHub repositories, posting stolen data and pulling down new commands or payloads. This is a form of 'Living off the Trusted Site' (LOTS).
- **Defense Evasion:** By using PowerShell and GitHub, the attack minimizes its footprint on the infected system and makes its network traffic appear benign to most security solutions.

---

## Technical Analysis

The attack unfolds in several stages:

1.  **Delivery:** A user receives a malicious LNK file, likely as an email attachment or a download from a compromised site.
2.  **Execution:** The user clicks the LNK file. The file is configured to execute a PowerShell command hidden in its 'Target' properties. To maintain the guise, it also opens a legitimate-looking decoy document.
3.  **C2 Communication:** The PowerShell script collects initial system information (e.g., hostname, username, IP address). It then makes API calls to GitHub, often creating or updating a file in a public or private repository controlled by the attacker, with the collected information encoded within.
4.  **Tasking:** The script periodically polls the GitHub repository for new instructions. The attacker can update a file in the repository with new PowerShell commands, which the implant will then download and execute.
5.  **Payload Delivery:** This C2 channel can be used to download further malware, such as Remote Access Trojans (RATs) like XenoRAT, for long-term control and more extensive data theft.

Recent versions of the malware have shown increased obfuscation, removing metadata that previously linked them to North Korean actors and hiding C2 instructions more deeply within the LNK file's arguments.

---

## Impact Assessment

The primary goal of this campaign is espionage. By gaining a persistent foothold within South Korean organizations, the attackers can:

- Collect political, military, and economic intelligence.
- Steal intellectual property and sensitive corporate data.
- Monitor communications of targeted individuals.
- Use the compromised networks as a launchpad for further attacks.

While not financially destructive in the way ransomware is, the long-term strategic damage from such intelligence gathering can be immense.

---

## Detection & Response

**Detection Strategies:**
1.  **PowerShell Logging:** Enable and monitor PowerShell script block logging (Windows Event ID 4104). Look for obfuscated PowerShell commands and scripts that make web requests to `api.github.com`. This is a core component of **[D3FEND Process Analysis](https://d3fend.mitre.org/technique/d3f:ProcessAnalysis)**.
2.  **Network Traffic Analysis:** While the traffic is encrypted (HTTPS), monitoring DNS queries and creating alerts for processes connecting to `github.com` or `api.github.com` that are not known developer tools or web browsers can be an effective detection method. This is an application of **[D3FEND Network Traffic Analysis](https://d3fend.mitre.org/technique/d3f:NetworkTrafficAnalysis)**.
3.  **Endpoint Analysis:** Scan for LNK files with unusual target paths, especially those that contain `powershell.exe` or `cmd.exe`. EDR solutions can be configured to alert on the execution of PowerShell from a LNK file shortcut.

**Response Actions:**
- If detected, block network access to the specific GitHub repositories being used for C2.
- Isolate the affected endpoint and analyze the PowerShell script to understand its full capabilities and what data may have been exfiltrated.
- Report the malicious repositories to GitHub for takedown.

---

## Mitigation

1.  **Block LNK Attachments:** Configure email gateways to block or quarantine emails with LNK file attachments, as they have few legitimate use cases in email.
2.  **Attack Surface Reduction (ASR) Rules:** Enable the ASR rule "Block execution of potentially obfuscated scripts" to prevent the execution of the malicious PowerShell.
3.  **User Training:** Educate users about the dangers of LNK files and to be suspicious of any unexpected document attachments.
4.  **Restrict PowerShell Execution:** Use PowerShell Constrained Language Mode where possible, which limits the dangerous capabilities that can be invoked by scripts.
